title: The adult with a huge spleen and unexplained bruising
blurb: The most common lysosomal storage disease — massive hepatosplenomegaly, low counts, and bone crises from lipid-laden macrophages; notably treatable.

patient.complaint: Easy bruising, fatigue, abdominal fullness, and deep bone pain.

opening: 27-year-old woman of Ashkenazi Jewish descent with months of easy bruising, fatigue, early satiety, and episodes of deep femoral bone pain. Exam shows massive splenomegaly and hepatomegaly. CBC: Hgb 9.5, platelets 70k. Marrow shows lipid-laden macrophages with a 'crumpled tissue paper' cytoplasm. There is NO neurological involvement. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Insidious onset over a year. Two prior 'bone crises' misdiagnosed as osteomyelitis. No cherry-red spot, normal cognition and development (this is the non-neuronopathic, type 1 form). Family of Ashkenazi descent (high carrier frequency).
exam: Pallor, scattered bruises. Massive splenomegaly (to the pelvis) and hepatomegaly. Point tenderness over the distal femur. No cherry-red macula, normal neuro exam. No lymphadenopathy.

prompts.cause: Which enzyme is deficient, and what substrate accumulates?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why hepatosplenomegaly and cytopenias (and why is type 1 NON-neuronopathic, unlike Tay-Sachs)?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the sphingolipid-degradation step that fails in Gaucher disease.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — β-glucocerebrosidase. Glucocerebroside accumulates in the lysosomes of reticuloendothelial macrophages (the spleen, liver, and marrow), forming Gaucher cells → hepatosplenomegaly, cytopenias (hypersplenism), and bone disease. Because storage is in macrophages (not neurons), the common type 1 form spares the CNS — and it is treatable with enzyme replacement.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The accumulating substrate is glucocerebroside — which lysosomal hydrolase normally clears it to ceramide?
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: β-Glucocerebrosidase (Glucocerebroside → Ceramide). Gaucher is the commonest lysosomal storage disease; type 1 is non-neuronopathic and treatable (ERT, substrate-reduction therapy).

mcqs.stem: Why does Gaucher cause hepatosplenomegaly and cytopenias, while Tay-Sachs does not?
choices: Glucocerebroside accumulates in reticuloendothelial macrophages (spleen/liver/marrow), causing organomegaly and hypersplenism; GM2 in Tay-Sachs accumulates in neurons
mcqs.explanation: Substrate distribution dictates phenotype. Glucocerebroside loads macrophages → big spleen/liver, low counts, bone disease (Gaucher cells). GM2 ganglioside is neuron-enriched, so Tay-Sachs is CNS-predominant with NO organomegaly. The pathway is shared; the storage site differs.

mcqs.stem: What is the 'crumpled tissue paper' cell, and why does it matter?
choices: The lipid-laden Gaucher macrophage, whose marrow presence helps confirm the diagnosis (definitive test is enzyme assay/genetics)
mcqs.explanation: Gaucher cells are engorged macrophages with a striated, crumpled-paper cytoplasm. They support the diagnosis, but the gold standard is reduced leukocyte β-glucocerebrosidase activity confirmed by GBA genetics.

mcqs.stem: What disease-specific treatments exist for type 1 Gaucher?
choices: Enzyme replacement therapy (recombinant glucocerebrosidase) and substrate-reduction therapy (e.g., miglustat/eliglustat)
mcqs.explanation: Type 1 Gaucher is one of the treatable lysosomal storage diseases: IV enzyme replacement reverses organomegaly and cytopenias, and oral substrate-reduction therapy lowers glucocerebroside production. Note GBA variants also raise Parkinson disease risk.
